title: Hall-Effect Speed Sensor for Rotating Machinery
content: A Hall-effect sensor outputs a digital pulse each time a magnet (or ferrous gear tooth) passes. Rotational speed is calculated as: ω (rpm) = (pulse_count / pulses_per_rev) × (60 / Δt), where Δt is the measurement window in seconds.
extended_content: Common Hall sensors: A3144 (unipolar, requires N-pole magnet), AH3503 (linear output), SS49E (ratiometric linear). For bicycle speedometers, a single spoke magnet gives 1 pulse/revolution. For higher resolution, multi-pole ring magnets or toothed steel discs with gap sensors (e.g., HS-M3G0) provide 12–60 pulses/revolution. Debouncing: digital Hall sensors have built-in Schmitt trigger, eliminating mechanical bounce. Interrupt-driven measurement minimises CPU load: capture μs timestamps on rising edge, compute period = t2 − t1, speed = 1 / (period × pulses_per_rev). Anemometer cups use the same Hall pulse-count principle (see Cup Anemometer fact). Speed range: 0–150 000 rpm typical for digital Hall sensors.
formula: ω [rpm] = (60 × f_pulse) / pulses_per_rev,  where f_pulse = pulse_count / Δt
source: Allegro Microsystems A3144 Hall-Effect Switch Datasheet; Melexis Hall Sensor App Note
